text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Julien Alfred has picked up St Lucia’s first ever gold medal by winning the women’s 100m, after Team GB racked up six medals.

Alfred stormed over the finishing line with a time of 10.72secs, while Team GB’s Daryll Neita came home in fourth.

Earlier Team GB defied the odds by winning gold in the men’s eight rowing event and bronze in the women’s event on the final day of rowing.

Emma Wilson then added windsurfing bronze in the women’s final having had to wait two days for her final due to wind-induced delays.

And the Team GBdressageteam then secured bronze thanks to Becky Moody and Jagerbomb – thehorsethat replaced Charlotte Dujardin’s Valegro – alongside Carl Hester’s Fame and Charlotte Fry’s Glamourdale.

Jake Jarman added a fourth bronze of the day in the men’s gymnastics floor final before Max Whitlock slipped out of the medals after his final ever routine on the pommel horse.

Followi‘s live blog for updates.

We are going to close this blog now after another exciting day at the Olympics, which saw Team GB pick up five medals including a gold and St Lucia’s Julien Alfred win the nation’s first ever gold.

Team USA have picked up a gold and new world record in 4x100m medley, while Britain finishes in 7th.

Great Britain’s relay team is made up of Kathleen Dawson, James Wilby, Duncan Scott and Anna Hopkin.

Chucking it down with rain, but it hasn’t dampened fans’ enthusiasm for the headline event, the women’s 100m final. Sha’Carri Richardson is beaten into second by Julien Alfred, whose progression has been incredible. Fifth at the last World Championships, now Olympic champion. Neita finished fourth, 0.04sec behind USA’s Melissa Jefferson.

St Lucia’s Julien Alfred takes the gold with a time of 10.72secs, while Team GB’s Daryll Neita comes in fourth and just misses out on the bronze.

Alfred has picked up a first ever gold Olympic medal for Saint Lucia, a tiny country which has a population of around 180,000.

I have seen Femke Bol win Olympic gold live. One of only 80,000 out of a population of 8.1bn. That last leg. Insane.

Team GB’s Daryll Neita is the only Brit who has made it through to the final.

Neitashould be in with a good chance of success here as she achieved the fourth-fastest time across the eight semis.

It’s absolutely electric in the Stade de France, as they roar Femke Bol past the Americans, who look genuinely gutted to have won silver.

A brilliant bronze for the Brits though. Amber Anning subbed in for the final and ran the second-fastest final leg of any team. She almost ran down her University of Arkansas team-mate Kaylyn Brown in the final metres, but the Americans hung on by 0.27 seconds.

A great run from Team GB – but the big shock is Femke Bol of the Netherlands overtaking the US at the death to take gold. Wow.

Team GB’s fifth bronze of the day.

800m to go – the USA are in the lead but Team GB chasing.

Team GB were strong in the heats and should have a decent medal chance here.

The mixed 4x400m relay has just begun at the Stade de France – Samuel Reardon, Laviai Nielsen, Alex Haydock-Wilson and Amber Anning the four British runners.

I am a woman. The words of Imane Khelif herself, spoken with passion and sincerity inthe most trying of circumstances.

Whatever the rights and wrongs ofthe gender controversy that has engulfed her at these Olympic Games, or whatever others might say about her, she is unequivocal in this belief.

Despite the provocation, she has behaved with dignity throughout the storm, none of which is of her making.

Read Kevin’s full analysis of Khelif’s fighthere

Farcical scenes at the shot put, the athletes are slipping all over the place after the rain has fallen.

Nigeria’s Chukuwuebuka Enekwechi ended up on his backside.

Sam Reardon is one of the four Brits lining up in the mixed 4x400m relay final shortly.

This is his fascinating story after he lost his ‘biggest support and best friend’ in 2019.

Read James’ interview with Samhere.

Having swam in the heats, he is out of the 4x100m relay final which gets going just before 9pm.

Kathleen Dawson, James Wilby, Anna Hopkin and Duncan Scott will be the Team GB lineup as they take on the favourites USA and Leon Marchand’s French team.

Daryll Neita makes sure GB have an interest in the women’s 100m final.

She runs a 10.97sec to end up second in her heat.

It’s all down to Daryll Neita if GB are going to have a representative in the women’s final. She’s on the track now fine-tuning her starting blocks.

Just behind her, the men’s shot put is progressing. Ryan Crouser, the world record holder from the US, is way out in front.

Imani Lansiquot will also miss out on the 100m final, finishing fifth in her semi-final in 11.21sec.

Sha’Carri Richardson of the US was second behind St Lucia’s Julien Alfred, who ran a very impressive 10.84sec.

It’s all down to Daryll Neita if GB are going to have a representative in the final. She’s on the track now fine-tuning her starting blocks.

Just behind her, the men’s shot put is progressing. Ryan Crouser, the world record holder from the US, is way out in front.

Dina Asher-Smith seems baffled by that result.

“The race wasn’t even fast. I’m very disappointed. I just felt great,” she says. “I’m just disappointed because I’m in great shape. I’ve been running great all season. I fully expected to make that final.

“I know I’m in a lot better shape than that. I’m excited to attack the 200m but very disappointed. I fully should have made that.”

The second Brit in the 100m semi-final is Imani Lansiquot. Can she outperform Asher-Smith?

World champion and favourite Sha’Carri Richardson also in this second heat.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Alfred wins St Lucia's first ever gold medal as Team GB continue medal rush
author: George Simms
description: Follow's i's live blog from day eight of the Olympic Games in Paris
